questions.question: The CPU is the most important part of the computer. It is called:
questions.explanation: Page 491: The Central Processing Unit or CPU is the most important part of the computer. It is called the brain of the computer.

questions.question: The CPU can be divided into three main components:
questions.explanation: Page 491: The CPU can be divided into three main components: (a) ALU, (b) CU and (c) Registers.

questions.question: The Arithmetic and Logic Unit (ALU) performs:

options.B: All the mathematical and logical operations on the information supplied to the CPU

questions.explanation: Page 491: ALU performs all the mathematical and logical operations on the information supplied to the CPU.

questions.question: Control Unit (CU) directs the working of the CPU by fetching instructions from:
questions.explanation: Page 491: Control Unit directs the working of the CPU. It fetches instructions (Programs) from the memory.

questions.question: Registers are storage locations that hold instructions or data while the CPU is using them. They consist of:

options.B: Flip-flops and registers used by the CPU

questions.explanation: Page 491: Registers consist of flip-flops and registers used by the CPU. They are the fastest memory elements in the computer.

questions.question: CPU registers are the fastest memory elements in:
questions.explanation: Page 491: The registers used by the CPU are the fastest memory elements in the computer.

questions.question: Data Width refers to:

options.B: Number of bits of data that can be manipulated within the CPU at one given time

questions.explanation: Page 491: Data Width: It refers to the number of bits of data that can be manipulated within the CPU at one given time.

questions.question: The data width of a computer is also called its:
questions.explanation: Page 491: The data width of a computer is also called its word size.

questions.question: Computers have data widths ranging from:
questions.explanation: Page 491: Computers have data widths ranging from 8 to 64 bits.

questions.question: A higher data width means the CPU is capable of processing data:
questions.explanation: Page 491: A higher data width means the CPU is capable of processing data faster. A CPU with higher data width is more powerful.

questions.question: Address Range refers to the amount of memory that can be:

options.B: Directly read or written by the CPU

questions.explanation: Page 491: Address range refers to the amount of memory that can be directly read or written by the CPU.

questions.question: The speed of CPU is known as:
questions.explanation: Page 491: Clock Speed: The speed of CPU is known as Clock Speed.

questions.question: The computer is essentially composed of tiny devices that can be put on or off to indicate:
questions.explanation: Page 491: The computer is essentially composed of tiny devices that can be put on or off to indicate 1 or 0.

questions.question: To synchronize the change of all components, the CPU uses:
questions.explanation: Page 491: To synchronize the change of all these components the CPU uses an internal clock.

questions.question: The memory unit stores all instructions and data for:
questions.explanation: Page 491: The memory unit stores all instructions and data for the CPU.

questions.question: Memory is of two kinds:
questions.explanation: Page 491: Memory is of two kinds: (a) Primary and (b) Secondary.

questions.question: Primary Memory is often referred to as the working memory or:

options.B: Main memory of a computer system

questions.explanation: Page 491: Primary Memory is often referred to as the working memory or the main memory of a computer system.

questions.question: Primary memory is temporary in nature, i.e. Data stored in primary memory is lost when:
questions.explanation: Page 491: Primary memory is temporary. Data is lost when computer is switched off. So it is also called volatile memory.

questions.question: Primary memory is also called volatile memory. Example of primary memory is:
questions.explanation: Page 491: Primary memory is volatile. Example of primary memory is RAM.

questions.question: The two basic kinds of primary memory are:

options.B: Random Access Memory (RAM) and Read Only Memory (ROM)

questions.explanation: Page 491: Two basic kinds of primary memory are Random Access Memory (RAM) and Read Only Memory (ROM).

questions.question: RAM is a:
questions.explanation: Page 491: The RAM is a read/write memory. The CPU can change the contents of the RAM at any time.

questions.question: RAM is volatile, which means:

options.B: Data is lost when power is switched off

questions.explanation: Page 491: RAM is volatile. In addition, RAM is volatile - data lost when computer switched off.

questions.question: RAM capacity greatly influences the:
questions.explanation: Page 491: The RAM capacity greatly influences the computing ability of the computer. Capacity usually measured in kilobytes and megabytes.

questions.question: The ROM can NOT be:
questions.explanation: Page 491: The ROM can not be altered. Information stored on ROM at time of manufacture.

questions.question: Information is stored on the ROM at the time of its:
questions.explanation: Page 491: Information is stored on the ROM at the time of its manufacture.

questions.question: The ROM is non-volatile and retains its information even after:
questions.explanation: Page 491: The ROM is non-volatile and retains its information even after the power is turned off.

questions.question: PROM stands for:
questions.explanation: Page 491: PROM (Programmable Read Only Memory) has the option of being programmed.

questions.question: Cache is the primary memory that stores:
questions.explanation: Page 491: Cache is the primary memory that stores temporary data for quick access.

questions.question: Secondary Memory is used to store data for:
questions.explanation: Page 491: Secondary Memory: It is used to store data for a long term. It operates at a much slower rate than primary memory.

questions.question: Secondary memory is permanent in nature, so it is also called:
questions.explanation: Page 491: Secondary memory is permanent in nature, so it is also called non-volatile. It is also cheaper than primary memory.

questions.question: Common examples of secondary memory include:

options.B: Floppy diskettes, hard discs, magnetic tapes, CDs, DVDs, Memory Cards, USB Sticks

questions.explanation: Page 491: Common examples of secondary memory are floppy diskettes, hard (fixed) discs and magnetic tapes, CDs, DVDs, Memory Cards, USB Sticks.

questions.question: Secondary memory is NOT directly accessible to the CPU. Information is moved from secondary to:

options.B: Primary memory first and then to the CPU

questions.explanation: Page 491: It is not directly accessible to CPU. Information is moved from secondary memory to primary memory first and then to CPU.

questions.question: A floppy diskette is a plastic disk coated with:
questions.explanation: Page 491: A floppy diskette is a plastic disk coated with magnetic material.

questions.question: Special devices known as disk drives are capable of reading from and writing to floppies using:
questions.explanation: Page 491: Special devices known as disk drives are capable of reading from and writing to floppies using special magnetic 'head'.

questions.question: Magnetic tapes are long plastic tapes coated with:
questions.explanation: Page 491: Magnetic tapes are long plastic tapes coated with magnetic material.

questions.question: A problem with magnetic tapes is that information can NOT be:
questions.explanation: Page 491: A problem with magnetic tapes is that information can not be accessed directly as in floppy diskettes.

questions.question: The third type of medium, called fixed or hard disks, contains:

options.B: Several discs of a hard material

questions.explanation: Page 491: The third type of medium, called fixed or hard disks, are more or less similar to floppy. One hard disk drive contains several discs of hard material.

questions.question: Primary memory is fast but expensive. To reduce storage costs, computers also use:
questions.explanation: Page 491: Primary memory is fast but expensive. To reduce storage costs, computers also use secondary memory.

questions.question: Any piece of information stored on a floppy diskette can be:
questions.explanation: Page 491: Any piece of information stored on a floppy diskette can be directly accessed.

questions.question: The memory unit holds instructions and data:

options.B: Before or after the CPU processes these

questions.explanation: Page 491: The memory unit holds instructions and data before or after the CPU processes these.

questions.question: The Control Unit controls the flow of data between the ALU and:
questions.explanation: Page 491: Control Unit controls flow of data between the ALU and other parts of the computer.

questions.question: Primary memory must be able to provide data:
questions.explanation: Page 491: Primary memory is directly accessible to the CPU. It must be able to provide data very quickly.

questions.question: Primary memory is directly accessible to:
questions.explanation: Page 491: Primary memory is directly accessible to the CPU.

questions.question: The information on ROM might be in the form of crucial instructions that govern the:
questions.explanation: Page 491: Information on ROM might be crucial instructions that govern the working of the computer.

questions.question: In PROM, the manufacturer of computer may choose to load a program designed by his company. The computer would use this PROM like:
questions.explanation: Page 491: In PROM, manufacturer may load a program. The computer would use this PROM like any other ROM.

questions.question: Magnetic tapes can store far larger amounts of data than:
questions.explanation: Page 491: Magnetic tapes can store far larger amounts of data than the floppy diskette.

questions.question: It makes all the required calculations and processes data. This refers to:

options.B: CPU (it makes all the required calculations and processes data)

questions.explanation: Page 491: CPU makes all the required calculations and processes data.

questions.question: The Storage device of a computer system is known as:
questions.explanation: Page 491: The storage device of a computer system is known as memory.

questions.question: Memory Unit can receive data, hold it and deliver according to the instructions from:
questions.explanation: Page 491: Memory Unit can receive data, hold it and deliver according to the instructions from the control unit.

questions.question: With every tick of the internal clock, all switches change their position in:
questions.explanation: Page 491: With every tick of this clock all switches that need to change their position do so in perfect harmony.
